code:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Triple:
    subject: str
    predicate: str
    object: str

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-START

def predict(triples: list[Triple]) -> str:
    """
    Convert a list of triples about a monument into a single fluent sentence.
    """
    if not triples:
        return ""

    # Mapping from predicate to a natural‑language phrase.
    phrase_map = {
        "category": "belongs to the category",
        "country": "is located in",
        "location": "is located at",
        "municipality": "lies in the municipality of",
        "state": "is in the state of",
        "district": "is part of the district",
        "established": "was established on",
        "foundingDate": "was founded on",
        "owner": "is owned by",
        "owningOrganisation": "is owned by",
        "hasToItsNorth": "has to its north",
        "hasToItsSouthwest": "has to its southwest",
        "hasToItsWest": "has to its west",
        "hasToItsSoutheast": "has to its southeast",
        "inaugurationDate": "was inaugurated on",
        "capital": "has capital",
        "legislature": "has legislature",
        "dedicatedTo": "is dedicated to",
        "designer": "was designed by",
        "material": "is made of",
        "ethnicGroup": "has ethnic group",
        "language": "has language",
        "religion": "has religion",
        "nearestCity": "has nearest city",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "largestCity": "has largest city",
        "leader": "has leader",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "nativeName": "has native name",
    }

    # Group predicates by their subject to avoid repeating the subject.
    subject_map: dict[str, list[tuple[str, str]]] = {}
    for t in triples:
        phrase = phrase_map.get(t.predicate, t.predicate.replace('_', ' '))
        # Clean up object: strip surrounding quotes and extra spaces.
        obj = t.object.strip()
        if (obj.startswith('"') and obj.endswith('"')) or (obj.startswith("'") and obj.endswith("'")):
            obj = obj[1:-1]
        subject_map.setdefault(t.subject, []).append((phrase, obj))

    sentences = []
    for subj, preds in subject_map.items():
        # Build predicate parts.
        parts = [f"{phrase} {obj}" for phrase, obj in preds]
        if len(parts) == 1:
            clause = parts[0]
        else:
            clause = ", ".join(parts[:-1]) + ", and " + parts[-1]
        sentences.append(f"{subj} {clause}")

    # Join subject clauses into a single fluent sentence.
    if len(sentences) == 1:
        combined = sentences[0]
    else:
        combined = ", ".join(sentences[:-1]) + ", and " + sentences[-1]
    final_sentence = combined.rstrip() + "."
    # Capitalise first character.
    return final_sentence[0].upper() + final_sentence[1:]

# EVOLVE-BLOCK-END

The program must implement a function called 'predict' that accepts a list of triples and generates a coherent, contextually relevant sentence that accurately represents the information contained in the triples. Ensure that the generated text is fluent, grammatically correct, and maintains the meaning of the original data. There can be multiple triples given that make up a complex sentence. Converting all those triples into one sentence will award greater score. An example of a complex sentence:

(Antwerp | cityServed | Antwerp International Airport)
(Belgium | country | Antwerp)
(City of Brussels | capital | Belgium)
Example sentence:
"Antwerp International Airport serves the city of Antwerp which is in Belgium, where the capital is Brussels."

        "category": "belongs to the category",

        "municipality": "lies in the municipality of",
        "state": "is in the state of",
        "district": "is part of the district",
        "established": "was established on",
        "foundingDate": "was founded on",
        "owner": "is owned by",

        "nearestCity": "has nearest city",
        "currency": "uses currency",
        "largestCity": "has largest city",
        "leader": "has leader",
        "leaderTitle": "has leader title",
        "nativeName": "has native name",
    }

    # Group predicates by their subject to avoid repeating the subject.
    subject_map: dict[str, list[tuple[str, str]]] = {}
    for t in triples:
        phrase = phrase_map.get(t.predicate, t.predicate.replace('_', ' '))
        # Clean up object: strip surrounding quotes and extra spaces.
        obj = t.object.strip()
        if (obj.startswith('"') and obj.endswith('"')) or (obj.startswith("'") and obj.endswith("'")):
            obj = obj[1:-1]
        subject_map.setdefault(t.subject, []).append((phrase, obj))

    sentences = []
    for subj, preds in subject_map.items():
        # Build predicate parts.
        parts = [f"{phrase} {obj}" for phrase, obj in preds]
        if len(parts) == 1:
            clause = parts[0]
        else:
            clause = ", ".join(parts[:-1]) + ", and " + parts[-1]
        sentences.append(f"{subj} {clause}")
